title: Red Snapper Act of 2025
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.
plain_summary: This bill stops the government from closing fishing areas in the South Atlantic Ocean until scientists finish a big study counting red snapper fish. The ban on closures applies to fishing for red snapper, grouper, and porgy until the study is done and its results are used in official reports about fish populations. The bill aims to protect fishing businesses that bring billions of dollars to the South Atlantic region. Fishermen believe red snapper numbers have gotten better based on what they see when they go fishing.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Red Snapper Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Conditions for Rules and Secretarial Amendments Related to Certain Area Closures
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. Fishing brings a lot of money to the South Atlantic area. In Florida alone, people who fish for fun bring in fourteen billion dollars and create one hundred nineteen thousand jobs. Red snapper is a fish that both fun fishers and business fishers really want to catch. In twenty eighteen, just six days of red snapper fishing season brought thirteen million dollars to the South Atlantic area's economy. Since twenty ten, the people who manage fishing have been working to bring back more red snapper fish in the South Atlantic. Right now there are more red snapper than ever before and many young fish are joining the group. Because there are so many red snapper now, fishers are catching them and throwing them back during times when fishing for them is not allowed. This throwing back is causing red snapper to die. Even with these fish dying from being thrown back, the South Atlantic Fishery Management Council's group of fishing experts believes that based on what they see while fishing, the red snapper population has recovered. However, some ideas to reduce the catching and throwing back of red snapper include making fishing seasons shorter and closing large areas where people fish for snapper and grouper in the South Atlantic. The State of Florida is worried about how closing fishing areas would hurt the economy of people who fish for fun in the South Atlantic snapper and grouper fishery. Eight million seven hundred thousand dollars has been spent over the last three years on independent survey research, including three million three hundred thousand dollars for the South Atlantic Great Red Snapper Count. This count will estimate how many red snapper live in South Atlantic waters from North Carolina to Florida. The National Marine Fisheries Service should use information from this survey in their fish population studies as quickly as possible to make better decisions about managing fishing. The Secretary of Commerce cannot make any temporary or permanent rule or official change that closes any area or bottom area in the South Atlantic for fish species managed under the Fishery Management Plan for the Snapper Grouper Fishery of the South Atlantic Region until two things happen. First, the South Atlantic Great Red Snapper Count study must be finished. Second, the information from that study must be included in the first South Atlantic red snapper Southeast Data Assessment and Review fish population study that happens after this law is passed.
